did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning, specifically a hybrid method combining balanced mini-batch logic, real-time image augmentation, and a customized loss function with an EfficientNetB4 deep CNN architecture, to perform multi-class skin-disease classification on dermoscopic images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a hybrid method for skin-disease classification using a customized loss function, balanced mini-batch logic, and real-time image augmentation. It details the development of the EfficientNetB4-CLF model, describes the training dataset of 24,530 images, and reports specific performance metrics (e.g., 89.97% accuracy) on a test dataset, which are characteristics of an original research study rather than a review.

title: Improving Skin-Disease Classification Based on Customized Loss Function Combined With Balanced Mini-Batch Logic and Real-Time Image Augmentation
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of class imbalance in multi-class skin-disease classification by proposing a hybrid method that combines balanced mini-batch logic with real-time image augmentation at the data level and a customized loss function at the algorithm level. The researchers trained their EfficientNetB4-CLF model on a large dataset of 24,530 dermoscopic images across seven skin disease categories. Performance was evaluated on a test set of 2,453 images, comparing the proposed approach against original methods and top-performing models from the ISIC 2018 challenge. The EfficientNetB4-CLF model achieved the highest accuracy of 89.97% and a mean recall of 86.13%, significantly outperforming baseline models in both metrics. Additionally, the method reduced the standard deviation of recalls to 7.60%, demonstrating improved balance in performance across minority classes compared to the majority classes. These results indicate that the proposed hybrid approach effectively mitigates class imbalance issues in deep convolutional neural networks for dermatological diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: Malignant class; 448 images in the test set; highest mortality rate skin cancer.
Benign class; 1,281 images in the test set; often confused with melanoma.
Malignant class; 331 images in the test set.
Malignant class (grouped with melanoma and BCC); 85 images in the test set; lowest recall in original models.
Benign class; 259 images in the test set.
Benign class; 24 images in the test set; lowest recall in original models.
Benign class; 25 images in the test set.

all_task_types.target: Seven skin disease categories (Melanoma, Melanocytic Nevus, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratosis, Benign Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Vascular Lesion)
all_task_types.details: Multi-class classification of dermoscopic images into discrete diagnostic categories. The study addresses class imbalance in multi-class skin-disease classification using an EfficientNetB4-CLF model. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, mean recall, mean precision, and standard deviation of recalls on a test set of 2,453 images.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, originally containing 10,000 images.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019
datasets_used.description: Additional images from the ISIC 2019 dataset added to the HAM10000 dataset to increase sample size.

use.training: Images added to the training/validation/testing pool.
use.validation: Images added to the training/validation/testing pool.
use.testing: Images added to the training/validation/testing pool.

datasets_used.name: Custom Combined Dataset (Train-80, Validation-10, Test-10)
datasets_used.description: The final dataset used in the study, formed by combining HAM10000 and ISIC 2019 images, removing duplicates, and splitting them into training, validation, and test sets with an 80/10/10 ratio. Images were center cropped and resized to 256x192px.

use.training: Used for training the EfficientNetB4 and DenseNet169 models with balanced mini-batch logic and real-time augmentation.
use.validation: Used for selecting the final model (highest mean recall on Validation-10).

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: AVG-MEL-NV (Average recall of Melanoma and Melanocytic Nevus)
DEV-MEL-NV (Deviation recall between Melanoma and Melanocytic Nevus)

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To measure the spread or imbalance of recall values across different classes, with lower values indicating more balanced performance.
To evaluate the average performance across all classes, specifically to address class imbalance issues.
To evaluate the average precision across all classes.

statistical_methods_used.method: AVG-MEL-NV (Average recall of Melanoma and Melanocytic Nevus)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To specifically evaluate the performance on the two majority classes.

statistical_methods_used.method: DEV-MEL-NV (Deviation recall between Melanoma and Melanocytic Nevus)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the balance of performance between the two majority classes.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive statistics such as accuracy, mean recall, mean precision, and standard deviation of recalls.

Custom Combined Dataset (Test-10).description: Test set of 2,453 images derived from HAM10000 and ISIC 2019, split 10% of total 24,530 images.

EfficientNetB4-CLF.notes: Highest accuracy and mean recall among all tested models. Reduced stdev by 4.24% compared to EfficientNetB4-ORI (from 11.84% to 7.60%). Increased mRecall by 4.65% compared to EfficientNetB4-ORI (86.13% vs 81.48%).

DenseNet169-CLF.notes: Second highest mean recall. CLF scenario improved mRecall over ORI and BON for both architectures.

EfficientNetB4-ORI.notes: Baseline for EfficientNetB4. MEL misclassification to NV was 17.6% + 3.3% = 20.9%.

EfficientNetB4-BON.notes: BON scenario provided best mRecall during validation epochs for EfficientNetB4, but final test results for CLF were superior.

DenseNet169-BON.notes: BON scenario improved recall for minority classes AK and DF compared to ORI.

ISIC 2018 TOP-1.notes: Ensemble CNN. EfficientNetB4-CLF outperformed in mRecall (86.13% vs 83.36%) and stdev (7.60% vs 9.76%).

ISIC 2018 TOP-7.notes: Single CNN based on DenseNet. EfficientNetB4-CLF outperformed in mRecall (86.13% vs 78.93%) and stdev (7.60% vs 9.35%). DEV-MEL-NV was 16.8% for TOP-7.

Custom Combined Dataset (Test-10).notes: The EfficientNetB4-CLF model achieved the highest accuracy (89.97%) and mean recall (86.13%). It reduced the standard deviation of recalls to 7.60%, indicating better balance across minority classes compared to baselines. The study compared these results against ISIC 2018 top methods (TOP-1 and TOP-7).

architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning from ImageNet; CNN layers transferred from networks trained by ImageNet

architecture_details.feature_extractor_output: Output of last layers (relu with DenseNet169 and top_conv with EfficientNetB4) passed as input features
architecture_details.custom_layers: Custom Fully Connected Layers (CFCL) with two hidden layers, 1,024 nodes each, ReLU activation, Batch Normalization, and Dropout (rate 0.2)
architecture_details.output_layer: Fully connected layer for multi-class classification (7 classes)
architecture_details.optimizer: Adam (implied by context of standard deep learning frameworks and CyclicLR, though specific optimizer name not explicitly stated, CyclicLR is used for learning rate)
architecture_details.augmentation: Real-time image augmentation applied in each iteration of learning

training_details.loss_function: Custom Loss Function (CLF) based on mean squared error of each class with a coefficient to adjust accuracy
training_details.data_handling: Balanced Mini-Batch Logic combined with real-time image augmentation
training_details.validation_strategy: Model with highest mean recall on Validation-10 set selected as final model

preprocessing_techniques: Real-time image augmentation (applied in each iteration of learning via deep learning framework functions)
Balanced mini-batch logic (for sample selection during training)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied in each iteration of learning through deep learning framework functions to prevent overfitting and improve generalization, particularly for limited or imbalanced data. Images were selected by balanced mini-batch logic before being augmented.

conclusions: The proposed hybrid method, combining balanced mini-batch logic, real-time image augmentation, and a customized loss function, is highly effective in addressing class imbalance in multi-class skin-disease classification.

The EfficientNetB4-CLF model achieved the highest accuracy (89.97%) and mean recall (86.13%) on the test dataset of 2,453 images, outperforming baseline models and top-performing methods from the ISIC 2018 challenge.

The proposed solution significantly reduced the standard deviation of recalls to 7.60%, indicating improved performance balance across minority classes compared to majority classes, with a reduction of 4.24% in standard deviation compared to the original EfficientNetB4 model.

The hybrid approach effectively mitigates the problem of slow learning in minority classes by combining data-level methods (balanced mini-batch logic and real-time augmentation) with algorithm-level methods (customized loss function).

The EfficientNetB4 architecture combined with the proposed methods demonstrated better balance and efficiency on the validation dataset compared to DenseNet169, particularly in terms of the deviation between accuracy and mean recall.

The proposed EfficientNetB4-CLF model showed superior performance in both average recall (88.4%) and balance (DEV-MEL-NV of 13.8%) for the majority classes (Melanoma and Melanocytic Nevus) compared to ISIC 2018 TOP-1 and TOP-7 methods.
important_remarks: The study relies on a custom combined dataset derived from HAM10000 and ISIC 2019, which may introduce population bias as these datasets are not fully representative of global skin diversity.

The dataset exhibits strong class imbalance, with Melanocytic Nevus (NV) comprising over 52% of images, while Dermatofibroma (DF) and Vascular Lesion (VASC) have very few samples (24 and 25 images in the test set, respectively).
Performance evaluation is limited to a single internal test set (Test-10); the paper does not report results on external, independent validation datasets, limiting generalizability assessment.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was performed; comparisons rely on descriptive statistics like mean recall and standard deviation.
The study focuses on dermoscopic images only, limiting clinical relevance to settings where dermoscopy is available, rather than naked-eye visual inspection.
The proposed method addresses class imbalance but does not explicitly address potential data leakage risks from combining HAM10000 and ISIC 2019, though duplicates were removed.
The model's performance on minority classes (AK, DF, VASC) remains a challenge, with DF having the lowest recall in original models, indicating potential residual bias despite the hybrid method.
